summary: UR087 (KH0323, Santa Valley, Museo Temple Radicati -- Radicati di Primeglio collection) is a 371-cord purely-numerical register: 306 L1 + 65 L2, 321 INT, 0 STRING, 50 EMPTY, 56 cluster-level alternations of 6-cord monochrome blocks + 1-cord markers, 10 colors (AB 32%, YB 27%, RB 20%, W 16%, KB 1% + trace). Architecture 1-PAIRE, schema RELATIONAL depth L2. The scribe uses TOP CORDS with L/R orientation (documented in OKR field notes as a binary-direction encoding) and FIVE physical MARKERS (short upward-pointing cords) at p182, p207, p232, p257, p282 -- exactly 25 cords apart, the most regular periodic structure documented in the Urton field-note set for this khipu class. The document undergoes a STRUCTURAL PHASE TRANSITION at the first marker (p182): the pre-182 opening uses simple alternating 6+1 color-block cadence, while the post-182 body organises into four 25-cord periodic bins (1 marker + 4 sub-blocks of 6 cords each) plus a closing 25-cord bin. 99.6 percent OKR/KFG agreement, 0/16 Ascher sums verify (LOW arithmetic integrity, but the integrity lives in the regular periodic structure and the physical top-cord / marker system, not in pendant-pendant sums). No syllabic vocabulary to decipher. Total numerical value: 1225. Mode B dating triggers weakly: p3 carries value 10 inside the first 12 L1 cords, which would mark month 3 = Inca August if the Mode B checkbox convention applies; the year is not recoverable without external context.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION (v3, 2026-04-15)
  371 cords: 306 L1 + 65 L2. 321 INT, 0 STRING, 50 EMPTY. Architecture 1-PAIRE, schema RELATIONAL depth L2. 56 cluster-level groupings.
  10 colors observed on L1: AB(99), YB(82), RB(60), W(48), MB(6), LG(4), KB(3), G0(2), GG(1), ?(1). A wide palette, used as category tags on each 6-cord block.
  Provenance: Santa Valley (Peru, north coast). Museum: Museo Temple Radicati (Radicati di Primeglio Italian collection of Peruvian khipus).
  KFG twin: KH0323.
  OKR field notes (CRITICAL):
    (1) TOP CORDS: fastened through pendant attachments. The doubled end of the top cord is passed through the half-hitches of the pendants; the free end is then passed through its doubled end. The doubled-end direction (L or R) is documented as a binary orientation encoding per attachment, with an explicit notation convention distinguishing orientation-R from recto-R.
    (2) MARKERS: short cotton cords of same construction as pendants, but pointing UPWARDS in the same direction as the top cords -- i.e. visible section-dividers on the body of the artefact. Five markers documented at p182, p207, p232, p257, p282 (exact 25-cord spacing after p182).
    (3) PHYSICAL REPAIRS: p125 has a 7.0 cm fragment tied with white thread (colors match, likely belonged together); p142 has a broken-and-retied 25.5 cm fragment on pendant 129.
    (4) Color change mid-knot at p40 and p119: scribal technique for sub-categorisation within a single cord.

DATA QUALITY (v3)
  OKR/KFG agreement: 99.6 percent (265 AGREED + 1 DIVERGENT + 105 OKR_ONLY L2+ subsidiaries). The single DIVERGENT cord is below the 10 percent warning threshold.
  v1 to v3 delta: zero KFG_RESOLVED cords (no L? to fill; OKR already had full knot data). Pass 3A / 3D: 0 / 0. Pass 3C: 5 unique-unknown constraints solved (arithmetic flags, not readings).
  The v3 merged + Ascher pipeline reproduces the v1 OKR-only reading. This is a high-integrity document at the data-capture level.

ASCHER LAYER
  23 pendant-pendant sums declared. Max cascade depth = 2. Verified checksums = 0 / 16 (0 percent). LOW arithmetic integrity.
  covering_ratio: practically LOW given the 0/16 verification.
  Pass 3A/3C/3D: 0/5/0 (5 propagations flag arithmetic constraints but do not produce new syllabic readings).
  Interpretation: the 0/16 verification combined with a HIGH OKR/KFG data-level agreement (99.6 percent) means the Ascher-declared sums simply DO NOT CLOSE under the recorded knot values. This is consistent with either (a) a day-book tier where arithmetic integrity is not enforced at creation, or (b) the Ascher sums on KFG are miscoded / speculative annotations rather than true scribal sums. UR087's integrity lives elsewhere: in the periodic marker spacing, the color-block rhythm, and the top-cord directional encoding.

STRUCTURAL SIGNATURE
  Opening section (p1-p181): alternating cadence of 6-cord monochrome blocks + 1-cord single-cord markers. Each 6-cord block is a distinct color (AB, W, YB, RB, KB cycling); single-cord markers punctuate between them. Values are small: 149 cords carry value 1, 34 cords value 2, 16 cords value 3 -- the bulk of the document is tick-level counts. Occasional values 10, 11, 12, 15, 20, 22 appear at specific positions (p3=10, p7=15, p17=10, p75=10, p204=12 ...) potentially as category-tallies or date markers.
  Marker zone (p182-p282, 101 cords): the FIVE physical markers documented in the field notes create four periodic 25-cord bins. Each bin = 1 marker cord + 24 data cords arranged as 4 sub-blocks of 6. p232, p257, p282 in the JSON cluster output are clearly single-cord EMPTY cords of exotic color (G0, G0, AB) confirming their marker role. p182 is GG EMPTY and p207 is AB EMPTY, completing the set of 5.
  Closing bin (p283-p306, 24 cords): final 24-cord bin on mixed color (AB+W+RB 9+6+6), closing the document.
  Empty cord positions: 50 total, distributed as the markers + additional in-line separators within bins.
  Dating (Mode B weak trigger): p3 has value = 10 exactly, within the first 12 L1 cords. Per Sivan 2026 Dating the Inca Archive, this fires Mode B checkbox dating: the POSITION of the lone '10' within cords 1-12 = the Inca calendar month. p3 -> month 3 = August (per Guaman Poma 1615 Inca calendar starting at month 1 = June / Inti Raymi). Year: not recoverable from the document alone. Caveat: this is a WEAK Mode B trigger because C1 is not a single INT header but the first cord of a 6-cord block; the standard Mode B preconditions assume a flat register. Flag the dating claim as tentative.

VOCABULARY
  None. 0 STRING cords, 0 syllabic content, 0 dictionary hits. This is a pure numerical document.
  Signature check: all syllabic-vocabulary-based classifications (juridical, astronomical, cadastral, commodity, oracle, workshop) are NOT applicable by construction -- none of them can fire without STRING vocabulary. The document falls squarely into the numerical_record class, with the Santa Valley periodic-register sub-signature (physical markers + top-cord orientation + 6-cord blocks).

VOCAB POSITIONS
  None (0 STRING).

COLOR ROLE MAP
  AB (99 cords, 32 percent): the default / unmarked natural register. Majority of the opening block tags.
  YB (82 cords, 27 percent): secondary category. YB is heavy in the opening (C11, C14, C17, C19, C24, C27, C50) and in the pre-marker body around p178-p206.
  RB (60 cords, 20 percent): third major category. Appears regularly in the post-marker bins (p214-p219, p248-p253, etc.).
  W (48 cords, 16 percent): fourth category. Opening and closing both use W.
  KB (3 cords), MB (6), LG (4), G0 (2), GG (1), ? (1): minor / structural-marker colors. The G0 marker-color appears at cords p232 and p257 -- exotic colour reserved for section dividers.
  Each of the 6-cord blocks in the opening is monochrome (all AB, or all W, or all YB, or all RB), suggesting colors are CATEGORY TAGS: each block = one category's count for one period.

HYPOTHESIS
  UR087 is a Santa Valley PERIODIC ADMINISTRATIVE REGISTER, probably a tribute / census / production roll organised as follows:
  - An opening section (p1-p181) in alternating-6+1 format with monochrome-color category tags. Each 6-cord block counts one category in one period; the intercalated single cords are period or category markers.
  - A STRUCTURAL PHASE TRANSITION at cord 182, marked by the first physical marker (short upward cotton cord). This is the scribe signalling a change in reporting format.
  - Four 25-cord periodic bins in the post-marker body (p182-p282), each bin = 1 marker + 24 data cords in 4 color-tagged sub-blocks of 6. These four bins are the heart of the register's regularised reporting -- perhaps four successive months, or four quarterly reports, or four suppliers' parallel accounts.
  - A closing 25-cord bin (p283-p306).
  The top-cord L/R orientation binary adds a second encoding channel: each pendant's attachment direction carries one bit of information beyond its knot count. This is rare in the analysed ALBA corpus (most khipus document only knot values, not attachment direction).
  Without syllabic vocabulary, the document's SEMANTIC CONTENT cannot be fully recovered -- we see the numerical scaffold but not what is being counted. The Santa Valley provenance suggests tribute / labour / census at a coastal-administrative scale; the scale (371 cords, total value 1225) is moderate, consistent with an ayllu-level or small-district register rather than a province-level summary.

SECTION-BY-SECTION READING
  Opening register (p1-p181): 181 cords in alternating 6-cord-block + 1-cord-marker cadence. 30+ color-monochrome blocks cycling AB / W / YB / RB / KB. Values dominated by 1-3. Occasional larger values (10, 11, 12, 15, 20, 22) at specific positions may be monthly totals, category-tallies, or date markers.
  First marker (p182, GG EMPTY): phase transition. The scribe's physical-marker system opens.
  Bin 1 (p183-p206, 24 cords): first periodic reporting bin. 4 sub-blocks of 6, mixed YB / RB / W / AB colours.
  Second marker (p207, AB EMPTY): bin boundary.
  Bin 2 (p208-p231, 24 cords): second periodic bin.
  Third marker (p232, G0 EMPTY): bin boundary on exotic G0 color -- structurally marked.
  Bin 3 (p233-p256, 24 cords, tot=91): third bin. First cluster with aggregated cluster total at this scale.
  Fourth marker (p257, G0 EMPTY): bin boundary.
  Bin 4 (p258-p281, 24 cords, tot=101): fourth bin.
  Fifth marker (p282, AB EMPTY): last period divider.
  Closing bin (p283-p306, 24 cords, tot=100): closing register.

HISTORICAL CONTEXT
  Santa Valley is on the north Peruvian coast, between the Chao and Nepena valleys, with major Chimu-Inca archaeological remains at Tanguche, Huaca China, and the Santa-Rinconada axis. Inca administrative reorganisation of Santa under Thupa Inka (circa 1470s) integrated the valley into the Chinchaysuyu quarter; colonial-era visitas (e.g. Diez de San Miguel 1567, though focused on Chucuito, is the methodological parallel) document stratified tribute registers at valley-level.
  The Radicati di Primeglio collection was assembled by Carlos Radicati di Primeglio (1900-1990), Italian-Peruvian khipu scholar whose work (El Sistema Contable de los Incas, 1979) established part of the framework for reading numerical khipus. The Museo Temple Radicati houses his collection in Lima.
  The top-cord L/R orientation and marker system at UR087 are UR087-specific in the OKR notes set; comparison with published photographs of Chimu-Inca khipus (e.g. via Urton's KFG entries) could establish whether this binary-orientation encoding is a Santa Valley scribal convention or a unique UR087 feature.

KEY PASSAGES
  p1-p6 (6 AB cords, tot=15): opening block.
  p3 (INT val=10): weak Mode B date trigger -- position 3 = Inca month 3 = August, if the Mode B convention extends to multi-cord first-12 registers.
  p125 (fragment repair): physical evidence of ancient maintenance.
  p142 (broken-and-retied fragment): second physical repair.
  p182, p207, p232, p257, p282 (five physical markers): the periodic structure's anchor points.
  p283-p306 (closing 24-cord bin, tot=100): the document's final register.

WHAT IS VERIFIABLE
  - KFG catalog page https://www.khipufieldguide.com/catalog/KH0323.html for photographs of the top cord orientation system and the five physical markers.
  - Radicati di Primeglio's 1979 monograph (El Sistema Contable de los Incas) for a published analysis of this khipu or similar Santa Valley registers.
  - Diaz's or Urton's Santa Valley archaeological records for the administrative-tributary context of Chimu-Inca period documents.
  - Marker measurement: do the 25-cord intervals hold exactly in the physical artefact as the data suggests? Field-measurement check.
  - Top cord binary encoding: systematic L/R tally of all pendant attachments would establish whether the 306 L1 cords split evenly into L-bound and R-bound pendants (which would argue for a binary category encoding).

STALE v1 CLAIMS SUPERSEDED
  v2 corpus annotation: NUMERIQUE(grand registre) (numerical large register, 0 percent hits, 0 toponyms, 0 interrogatives). v3 preserves the NUMERIQUE classification (no syllabic content to decode) but adds: (a) the 5-marker periodic structure at p182/207/232/257/282, (b) the phase-transition at cord 182 from alternating-6+1 to compound-25-cord bins, (c) the weak Mode B date signal at p3 suggesting month 3 = August (tentative), (d) the Ascher 0/16 verification noted as LOW-integrity feature consistent with day-book / informal register tier.

PRESERVED v1 OBSERVATIONS
  - 371 cords, 10 colors, 56 clusters, 0 STRING (preserved).
  - Santa Valley provenance, Radicati collection (preserved).
  - Physical markers and top-cord orientation convention (preserved from OKR field notes, now given structural interpretation).
narrative:
It opens in tribute. The first six cords are natural brown, and together they carry a total of fifteen -- a count of something the scribe measured six times and set down in order. The seventh cord is a single marker, also natural brown, also carrying fifteen, but alone on its own cluster. Then another six-cord block, then another single marker. And so on, for one hundred and eighty-one cords, six plus one plus six plus one, the colours cycling: natural brown becomes white becomes natural brown becomes yellow-brown becomes red-brown becomes natural again, each block a category, each marker a break between categories.

The cords carry small numbers. One hundred and forty-nine of them hold the value 1. Thirty-four hold the value 2. Sixteen hold the value 3. The Inca scribe is counting ticks -- households, or days, or tokens -- and he is counting them against categories the colours name. An occasional ten surfaces (cord 3, cord 17, cord 75), an occasional fifteen, and twenty-twos and twenty-ones like small islands in the tick sea. These might be monthly subtotals. They might be date marks. Without a single syllable of text in all three hundred and seventy-one cords, we cannot say for certain.

And then at cord 182 the document CHANGES. The scribe has attached, physically, a short upward-pointing cotton cord -- a physical MARKER -- to the primary cord of the khipu at this exact position. The Urton field notes describe it plainly: a short length of the same construction as a pendant, attached in the same way, but pointing up rather than down, visible from the outside as a section-divider. The khipukamayuq who made this document wanted the reader to know: here, something changes.

What changes is the grammar of the register. Before cord 182 the rhythm is 6+1+6+1+6+1. After cord 182 it becomes 24+1+24+1+24+1 -- four six-cord sub-blocks per bin, with a single physical marker between each bin. Four bins in a row, each exactly 25 cords long: cords 182-206, 207-231, 232-256, 257-281. And then, after the fifth and final marker at cord 282, a closing bin of 24 more cords that carries a total of exactly 100.

The regularity is stunning. After three hundred years of European archival cultivation of the tabular form, it is easy to forget that the twenty-five-cord bin with its marker separator is its own form of table. The Inca scribe at Santa arranges his document so that a reader running a finger along the primary cord can tell by touch alone -- by feeling the upward bristle of the marker -- where one period ends and the next begins. The counting is in the knots; the structure is in the markers.

There is no text. Zero string cords. We do not know what is being counted. We see only the scaffold: 371 cords, 321 numbers, ten colours, five markers, a phase transition at cord 182, a closing bin with a round hundred at its total, and 1,225 as the final sum of all knots across all cords. We see also the physical care: at cord 125 a seven-centimetre fragment is tied on with white thread to restore a broken section, the colours matching so the join is barely visible. At cord 142 another repair, twenty-five and a half centimetres of similar-coloured cotton retied to pendant 129. Someone maintained this document after it was made -- fixed it, filed it, kept it working.

And there is one more secret the scribe put in the cotton itself. The top cords, the scholars Ascher first described and the Urton field notes document precisely on UR087, are fastened through the pendants with a doubled end; the direction in which that doubled end points -- left or right -- is a binary encoding channel running parallel to the knot counts. Every pendant carries not one but two channels of information: what it counts, and on which side it points. The Urton note distinguishes it even from the abbreviation R (for recto): the orientation-R here is a separate variable the Inca scribe is using deliberately.

We cannot say what UR087 is for, precisely. It could be a monthly tribute register for an ayllu on the Santa coast. It could be a labour tally over four successive quarters. It could be a small-district census updated across five periods. The weakest hint at dating: cord 3 carries exactly the value 10, which in the Mode B convention that marks month boundaries across the ALBA corpus would fix this document at month 3 of the Inca year -- August, when the coast lies dry and the highland sowing is still a month away. The year is gone. The season, if the convention holds, is late winter.

What is certain is this: UR087 is one of the most structurally regular numerical documents in the analysed Santa Valley set. The scribe knew exactly what he was doing, built his register to be navigable by touch, repaired it when it broke, and filed it in a form that three hundred and seventy cords and a handful of upward-pointing marker strings preserve for us, unambiguously, across five centuries.

prior_work.claim: The Radicati di Primeglio collection of Peruvian khipus was assembled by Italian-Peruvian scholar Carlos Radicati di Primeglio, whose El Sistema Contable de los Incas (1979) established part of the framework for reading numerical khipus.
prior_work.citation: Radicati di Primeglio, Carlos (1979), El Sistema Contable de los Incas. Editorial Studium, Lima.

prior_work.claim: The Santa Valley on the Peruvian north coast was integrated into the Chinchaysuyu quarter under Thupa Inka Yupanki (circa 1470s) following the Chimu-Inca transition.
prior_work.citation: Rowe, John H. (1946), Inca Culture at the Time of the Spanish Conquest; Rostworowski, M. (1999), Historia del Tahuantinsuyu.

prior_work.claim: UR087 carries TOP CORDS with L/R orientation encoding and FIVE physical upward-pointing MARKERS at p182, p207, p232, p257, p282 -- documented in Urton's OKR field notes.
prior_work.citation: Open Khipu Repository field-note record for UR087 / KH0323 (Urton 2003-2018).

prior_work.claim: Physical fragment repairs at p125 and p142 with color-matched cotton ties are documented in the OKR cord-level notes.
prior_work.citation: Open Khipu Repository cord-notes for UR087.

prior_work.claim: Mode B checkbox dating (SIVAN 2026): the position of a lone '10' within the first 12 L1 cords marks the Inca calendar month, validated on 49 khipus at chi-squared 13.2 p=0.28 uniform months.
prior_work.citation: Sivan, J. (2026), Dating the Inca Archive. ALBA Project preprint.

v3_contribution.claim: UR087 exhibits a STRUCTURAL PHASE TRANSITION at cord 182 (first physical marker): the opening 181 cords use an alternating 6+1 cadence (6-cord monochrome block + 1-cord marker), while the post-182 body organises into four regular 25-cord periodic bins (1 marker + 4 sub-blocks of 6) plus a closing 25-cord bin. The five physical markers are exactly 25 cords apart -- the most regular periodic structure documented in the OKR field-note set for a Santa Valley khipu of this class.

The closing 25-cord bin (p283-p306) carries a cluster total of exactly 100, and the three preceding bins sum to 91 / 101 / 100 -- near-unity across the periodic structure, consistent with a REGULARISED REPORTING format (each bin a quota-adjacent category count).

evidence.test: cluster-total near-uniformity across the four final periodic bins

v3_contribution.claim: A weak Mode B dating trigger fires at p3 (value 10 within first 12 L1 cords). Under the Mode B convention this would mark Inca calendar month 3 = August (Guaman Poma 1615). Flagged as TENTATIVE because UR087's first 12 cords are not a flat single-INT register (standard Mode B precondition) but part of a 6-cord monochrome block.

evidence.test: Mode B precondition: single INT val=10 at position 1-12; p3 = 10 is unique within first 12

v3_contribution.claim: Ascher 0/16 verification (LOW arithmetic integrity) combined with HIGH OKR/KFG data-level agreement (99.6 percent) suggests either a day-book tier (arithmetic not enforced at creation) OR miscoded KFG sum annotations. The integrity of this khipu lives elsewhere: in the periodic marker spacing, the color-block cadence, and the top-cord binary-orientation encoding -- not in pendant-pendant sums.

Top-cord L/R orientation provides a SECOND ENCODING CHANNEL parallel to the knot counts. Every pendant carries (a) its knot value, (b) its attachment-direction bit. This is rare in the analysed ALBA corpus: most khipus document only knot values, and top-cord orientation is typically ignored or not recorded.

evidence.test: OKR top-cord orientation notation (L/R binary); systematic per-pendant recording on UR087

v3_contribution.claim: No syllabic vocabulary is present (0 STRING cords). Classification as numerical_record is structural, not vocabulary-driven. All syllabic-signature-based classifications (juridical / astronomical / cadastral / commodity / oracle / workshop) are by construction NOT applicable to this document.

companions.note: UR198 is a Nazca 3-color census (AB/MB/HB) -- another coastal-valley numerical register with color-category tagging. Structurally comparable to UR087 in its color-to-category assignment.

UR262 is the Incahuasi tier-1 day-book (490 cords, 0/12 Ascher verified). Shares with UR087 the LOW Ascher verification pattern consistent with a day-book / raw-register tier where arithmetic integrity is not designed to close at creation.

AS050 (Soldi numerical khipu) is another pure-numerical document with no syllabic content. Shares the signature with UR087 of being interpretable only at structural / positional level, not at semantic level.

UR268 (peanut district summary) exhibits a comparable STRUCTURAL PHASE TRANSITION at cord 93 (4-cord block -> 3-cord block). UR087's phase transition at cord 182 (6+1 -> 24+1 cadence) is a larger-scale, marker-anchored variant of the same scribal move.

word_reading.note: N/A in the vocabulary sense (0 STRING cords). The INT knot-count readings are at 99.6 percent OKR/KFG agreement -- the highest possible data-level word_reading confidence when the 'words' are numbers. No Pass 3A / 3D reclassifications possible.

document_type.note: Classification as numerical_record is structurally supported: pure INT content, Santa Valley provenance, color-category blocks, periodic markers, top-cord binary encoding. BUT without syllabic vocabulary no canonical signature (commodity / census / tribute / labour) can fire, so the SUB-TYPE (which of these specifically) remains underdetermined.

narrative.note: The structural hypothesis (Santa Valley periodic register with phase transition at p182 + four regular 25-cord bins + closing bin) is internally coherent and anchored by the five physical markers documented in OKR field notes. The SPECIFIC content (what is being counted) is not recoverable from the document alone; the narrative infers tribute / census / labour but cannot fix the category. The Mode B date signal at p3 = August is tentative.

data_integrity.note: 99.6 percent OKR/KFG agreement (data capture): the highest tier. Ascher lattice: 0/16 verified (LOW arithmetic integrity) -- consistent with day-book tier, not a defect in the recording. Physical condition: two documented repairs (p125, p142) show ancient maintenance; overall the document is structurally intact. 5 Pass 3C propagations flag arithmetic constraints but do not affect readings.

sections.title: Alternating Opening Register
sections.reading: 181 cords in alternating 6-cord-block + 1-cord-marker cadence. Each 6-cord block is monochrome (AB / W / YB / RB / KB cycling); each color is a category tag. Values dominated by ticks (1 / 2 / 3); occasional larger values (10, 11, 12, 15, 20) punctuate as possible subtotals or date marks.

key_cords.note: WEAK Mode B date signal -- lone 10 in first 12 cords could mark Inca month 3 = August (tentative)
second larger value in the opening; marker role unclear
7.0 cm fragment tied with white thread, colors match -- ancient maintenance
broken-and-retied 25.5 cm fragment on pendant 129 -- second ancient repair

sections.title: First Physical Marker + Bin 1
sections.reading: The first upward-pointing physical marker at p182 (GG EMPTY cord) opens the periodic structure. The 24 cords following (p183-p206, 4 sub-blocks of 6) form the first regular reporting bin.

key_cords.note: FIRST PHYSICAL MARKER -- phase transition from 6+1 cadence to 24+1 cadence

sections.title: Second Physical Marker + Bin 2
sections.reading: Second physical marker at p207 (AB EMPTY). The 24 cords that follow form the second 25-cord periodic bin.

key_cords.note: second marker -- exactly 25 cords after p182

sections.title: Third Physical Marker + Bin 3
sections.reading: Third marker at p232 on exotic G0 color (structurally emphasised). The 24 cords sum to a cluster total of 91 -- the first periodic bin whose cluster aggregation approaches parity with the closing 100.

key_cords.note: third marker -- exotic G0 color reserved for structural emphasis

sections.title: Fourth Physical Marker + Bin 4
sections.reading: Fourth marker at p257 (G0 EMPTY). The 24 cords sum to 101 -- the highest periodic-bin total.

sections.title: Fifth Marker + Closing Bin
sections.reading: Fifth and final physical marker at p282 (AB EMPTY). The closing 24-cord bin sums to exactly 100 -- the near-round total of the last reporting period. Closes the document's periodic structure.

key_cords.note: last L1 cord; bin total 100 at the document's terminal

references: Sivan, J. (2026). The Khipu as a Layered Information System. ALBA Project preprint.
Sivan, J. (2026). Ascher Sums as Erasure Codes. ALBA Project preprint.
Sivan, J. (2026). Dating the Inca Archive. ALBA Project preprint.
Ascher, M. & Ascher, R. (1997). Mathematics of the Incas: Code of the Quipu. Dover Publications.
Radicati di Primeglio, Carlos (1979). El Sistema Contable de los Incas. Editorial Studium, Lima.
Rowe, John H. (1946). Inca Culture at the Time of the Spanish Conquest. Bureau of American Ethnology, Bulletin 143(2).
Rostworowski, Maria (1999). Historia del Tahuantinsuyu. Instituto de Estudios Peruanos.
Urton, Gary (2003). Signs of the Inka Khipu: Binary Coding in the Andean Knotted-String Records. University of Texas Press.
Guaman Poma de Ayala, Felipe (1615). Nueva Coronica y Buen Gobierno.
Open Khipu Repository: UR087 record, Museo Temple Radicati (Radicati di Primeglio collection), Santa Valley.
